observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:


We recommended that  Management  enforce  effective  and  efficient  control
measures to ensure timely  turn-over  of  collections  from  collectors  and
tellers to the Municipal Treasurer. In view of this,  direct  the  Municipal
Treasurer to:


     1. Update the preparation of the RAAF taking note of accountable forms
        issued to collectors in  prior  years  which  are  still  in  their
        custody;


     2. Issue a demand letter to  collectors  with  unreturned  accountable
        forms;


     3. Stop the issuance of accountable forms to collectors who still have
        accountable forms in their custody;


     4. Stop the practice of  issuing  accountable  forms  to  unauthorized
        personnel;


     5. Require the use of a RIS to document the requisition  and  issuance
        of accountable forms; and


     6. Assign personnel to regularly update  the  logbook  of  accountable
        forms.
Further, direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit  all  collections  intact
and  regularly  to  the  AGDB  to  avoid  the  accumulation  of  undeposited
collections at month-end.


Management’s Comments:

The Municipal Treasurer justified that delays in deposit  were  due  to  the
distance between the agency and the authorized government  depository  bank,
also considering the risk in bringing significant amounts of cash. To  avoid
this, Management is considering opening an account with  BDO  Cateel  branch
for  the  deposit  of  collections,  should  this  be  authorized   by   the
appropriate  agency.  As  for  the  delayed  turnover  of   collection   and
monitoring of the issuance of accountable  forms,  the  Municipal  Treasurer
already issued Office Memorandum in January 2022 to the concerned  personnel
to address the said lapses.

Recommendation:

We recommended to direct the Municipal  Accountant  to  make  the  necessary
adjusting entries to reflect the correct cash in bank balance at  year  end,
and to ensure the timely preparation of the bank  reconciliation  statements
to ensure the prompt recording of reconciling items requiring adjustment  in
the books.

Management’s Comments:

During the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant  assured  us  that  the
pertinent adjusting entries will be recorded in the current  year.  Further,
the delay in the preparation of the BRS and  in  recording  the  reconciling
items was due to the delay of the bank in transmitting copies  of  the  bank
statements.

Recommendation:


We reiterated our recommendation  that  Management  enforces  the  immediate
liquidation of  the  funds  transferred  or  resort  to  legal  remedies  if
warranted, pursuant to Section 9 of COA Circular 2007-001.
Management’s Comments:

The Municipal Accountant has already issued a demand letter to the  CFP-TMPC
Treasurer on February 18, 2022 requiring the immediate liquidation  of  cash
advances for P337,356.76. Further, Management intends  to  resort  to  other
remedies should the CFP-TMPC fail to liquidate the cash advance despite  the
receipt of demand letter from the Accounting Office.

Recommendations:


We recommended that the MTO update its assessed  value  of  real  properties
based on the  assessment  roll  provided  by  the  Municipal  Assessor,  and
provide the Accounting Office with the certified list of  taxpayers  showing
the amount of tax due and collectible to serve as basis  in  setting-up  the
correct RPT/SET Receivables in the books.  Likewise,  require  the  MTO  and
Accounting Office to periodically reconcile  the  RPT/SET  Receivables  with
the reported delinquent taxes and make the necessary adjustments to  reflect
the correct balance.

Management’s Comments:

As justified by the Former OIC  Accountant,  one  of  the  factors  for  the
variance in the RPT/SET Receivables balance between Accounting and  Treasury
records is the failure of the Municipal  Accounting  Office  to  record  the
receivables for CYs 2013 to 2019. Further,   the  required  report  was  not
provided by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office, thus, the RPT/SET  Receivables
recognized in the books were  based  on  monthly  reports  provided  by  the
Municipal Assessor’s Office. Nevertheless, the Municipal Accountant  assured
us that the Accounting and  Treasury  records  pertaining  to  RPT  and  SET
Receivables shall be reconciled in the current year.

Recommendation:

We recommended that in subsequent transactions  MACCO  shall  refer  to  the
Revised Chart of Accounts as prescribed in COA  Circular  2020-004  for  the
description  of  account   titles   to   properly   record   the   financial
transactions.  Further, provide adjustments in the books  of  accounts  (for
improperly classified asset accounts) to properly classify the  balances  in
the financial statements.

Management’s Comments:

The necessary adjusting entries will be recorded in the current year.

Recommendation:

As a reiteration of the prior year’s audit recommendations,  we  recommended
to the Management the following courses of action:

1. Direct the Inventory/Property  Committee  to  conduct  an  effective  and
   complete physical inventory of all PPE  items  of  the  Municipality  and
   prepare the corresponding RPCPPE with remarks on  the  condition  of  the
   listed equipment.


2. Reconcile the results  thereof  with  the  available  accounting  records
   maintained by the Municipal Accounting  Office  to  determine  unrecorded
   properties and other  differences  for  further  validation.  Thereafter,
   submit a report on the results of  reconciliation  made  for  review  and
   verification.


3. Maintain PPE ledger cards and  property  cards  to  facilitate  efficient
   reconciliation of physical count versus accounting and property records.


4. Provide necessary adjusting entries in the books of  accounts  if  deemed
   necessary.

Management’s Comment:

Management explained that the Inventory Committee  is  yet  to  be  created.
Further,  the  Accounting  Office  explained  that  subsidiary  ledgers  are
maintained for every PPE account. Copies of the same were  provided  to  the
Property Officer and Chairman of the Inventory Committee  as  reference  for
the conduct of physical count.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

The Accounting Office is reminded that PPE Ledger Cards are not the same  as
the  Subsidiary  Ledgers  provided  in  their  accounting  system.  Further,
considering that the same observation was made in  prior  years,  management
is  reminded  that  the   continued   failure   to   implement   the   audit
recommendations exposes the agency’s properties to theft or loss  for  which
the head of  the  agency  is  immediately  and  primarily  responsible  for,
pursuant to Section 102 of PD 1445.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that Management expedite the transfer of  ownership  of
the aforesaid parcels of land in the name of  the  Municipality  to  protect
the interest of the government. Further, allocate the  necessary  funds  for
the payment of processing fees that may be incurred in the titling  thereof.

Management’s Comments:

Management committed to facilitate the titling of these  properties  in  the
current year and to provide funds therefor.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

Considering that the titles of all the lots allegedly owned  by  the  agency
based on the accounting records were not yet transferred to the LGU and  the
related documents as proof of ownership cannot be located, there is  a  need
to fast track the titling of these properties such that the  same  will  not
be subject to disputes or claims of other parties and to fully  protect  the
interest of the LGU of Cateel.

Recommendations:

We recommended to Management the following:

1. Require the Relief Distribution In-Charge  and  Municipal  Accountant  to
   submit immediately the complete Relief Distribution Sheets as proof  that
   all procured items were actually received by the  affected  families  and
   individuals;

2. Provide a written justification on the variances noted  in the number  of
   beneficiaries based on the RDS and on  the  unaccounted  food  items  for
   which receipt was not indicated in the RDS; and

3. Require the Relief Distribution In-Charge to impose stricter controls  in
   the distribution of relief goods particularly  in  the  documentation  by
   ensuring  that  the  required  information  in  the  RDS  are  completely
   provided, all the recipients affixed their signature in the RDS as  proof
   of receipt of the food items, and the  total  number  of  recipients  are
   reconciled with the expected number of beneficiaries.

Management’s Comments:

In his reply to the AOM, Mr. Allen V. Sillada  justified that  the  lack  of
necessary information in the RDS such as the address of  the  beneficiaries,
date of receipt of the relief goods and failure to  affix  their  signatures
in the RDs, was only an isolated case as instructions on  accomplishing  the
RDS were already disseminated beforehand to the purok leaders.  As  for  the
unsubmitted RDS, these are still in the custody of  purok  leaders  in  far-
flung sitios particularly in GIDA areas. It was  also  reiterated  that  the
relief goods were equally distributed among  all  the  recipients.  Further,
the corresponding proof of distribution of relief  goods  in  the  month  of
December 2021 is  yet  to  be  transmitted  to  the  Municipal  Accountant’s
Office.

Nonetheless, Management committed to implement the audit observation in  its
succeeding transactions involving the distribution of relief goods.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

Considering that the aforementioned lapses in  the  distribution  of  relief
goods were also observed in the prior year and reported in the Annual  Audit
Report CY 2020, we  contend  that  the  failure  to  provide  the  necessary
information in the RDS is not an “isolated case” but  is  in  fact  a  clear
indication of Management’s  failure  to  impose  stricter  controls  in  the
distribution of relief goods.  Further,  sufficient  justification  was  not
provided on the variances noted in the number of beneficiaries based on  the
RDS and on the unaccounted food items for which receipt  was  not  indicated
in  the  RDS.  The  lacking  RDS  were  also  not  yet  submitted,  thus,  a
significant portion of the relief  goods  still  cannot  be  accounted  for.
Finally, Management is again reminded  to  exercise  due  diligence  in  the
preparation of documentary  requirements  to  preclude  the  issuance  of  a
notice of suspension or disallowance as a result of incorrect or  misleading
information.

Recommendations:

      We recommended that Management:

    1. Adopt a proper  and  effective  inventory  and  supplies  management
       system for donations in-kind and maintain the reports and records as
       required in the COA Circular.


    2. Direct the Municipal Treasurer’s Office as the Property  and  Supply
       Unit of the agency to facilitate the issuance of an  Acknowledgement
       Receipt for In-Kind Donations for the 2 motor vehicles  and  prepare
       the   Property   Acknowledgement   Receipt   to   establish   proper
       accountability for these PPE.


    3. Direct the MHO and  MDRRMO  to  transmit  all  necessary  supporting
       documents relative to the donated motor vehicles  to  the  Municipal
       Accountant for the recording of the assets in the books of  accounts
       and  preparation  of  the  Property  Ledger   Cards.   The   related
       depreciation expense shall also be recorded in the books  to  fairly
       present the PPE balance.

Management’s Comments:

      A letter dated February 18, 2022 was already  sent  by  the  Municipal
Accountant to the concerned offices requesting the submission  of  necessary
documents for  the  recording  of  the  two  (2)  units  of  ambulance.  The
necessary adjusting entries will be recorded in the current year.

Recommendations:

      We recommended the following courses of action:

1.  Direct  the  Municipal  Engineering  Office  to   comply   and   rectify
   immediately  the  noted  deficiencies   on   projects   implemented   “by
   administration.”  For  projects  implemented  by  contract,  compel   the
   contractor to complete the works or effect  settlement.  Further,  direct
   the Municipal Engineering  Office  to  submit  a  monthly  status  report
   thereon until all have been fully resolved, for our evaluation.

2. Submit a written justification on the construction  of  footbridge  which
   was  implemented  not  in   accordance   with   the   POW,   questionable
   implementation of farm to market road projects, projects in excess of the
   ABC, and deficiencies noted in the procurement of  electrical  materials,
   for us to evaluate whether to suspend or  disallow  the  transactions  in
   audit.

3. Direct the Municipal Accounting  Office  and  the  Municipal  Engineering
   Office to submit the lacking documents for the construction of  the  drop
   in center.

4. Direct the project-in-charge of the electrification program to submit  an
   inventory of all electrical materials charged against the 20% development
   fund, copies of the RIS on its issuance, copies of approved plans or duly
   revised plans, certificate of completion and acceptance of completed
   projects under the electrification program, certificate of inspection by
   the agency’s authorized  engineer,  pictures before,  during and after
   construction, and such other documents as may be necessary during project
   implementation.

5. Require the contractor of the drop  in  center,  Edsal  Construction  and
   Supply, Inc. to refund the amount in excess of the contract cost, in  the
   absence of a duly approved change order or extra work order.

6. Require the Municipal Engineer to  conduct  periodic  inspection  of  all
   ongoing projects to ensure that these are in accordance with the POW.

7. Direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all claims  are  supported
   with complete documentation.

8. The Municipal Planning and Development Office  (MPDO)  shall  ensure  the
   correct preparation of the Annual Investment Plan, and  ensure  that  the
   programs and projects as proposed by the  Municipal  Development  Council
   (MDC) are specifically described in the AIP.

9. Direct the Bids and Awards  Committee  (BAC)  and  the  Municipal  Budget
   Office (MBO) to ensure that the preparation of the PPMP and  APP  are  in
   accordance with Section 7  of  R.A.  9184  to  facilitate  the  efficient
   conduct of procurement activities.

Management’s Comments:

      The Municipal Engineer gave the following updates regarding the status
of completion of the above projects:

1. Painting works for the Purok Center at Sitio Hebron, Brgy. Taytayan,
   Cateel was already completed as of Feb. 11, 2022.


2. Electrical works for the Drop In Center was completed by the supplier,
   Edsal Enterprises, as evidenced by photos taken on February 21, 2022.

      As for the construction of footbridge at Brgy. Abejod,  Cateel,  which
was not  in  accordance  with  the  approved  POW,  the  Municipal  Engineer
justified that the changes in the approved works  was  in  consideration  of
the request of the residents for a semi-permanent structure.  The  materials
which were already delivered but not used for  the  project  were  kept  for
future  improvements.  Moreover,  the  additional  cost  as  a   result   of
variations in the project was shouldered by the Municipal Mayor out  of  her
personal funds.  For  comparison  of  project  cost,  a  new  POW  was  also
submitted by the Municipal Engineer which showed an estimated  project  cost
of P303,985.00.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

      Justification was not provided for other  deficiencies  noted  in  the
implementation of 20% development fund PPAs, particulary the Farm to  Market
Road, Electrification  Program,  uncompleted  tiling  works  at  the  public
market vegetable stands and disbursements in excess of the  ABC,  thus,  may
warrant the issuance of a Notice of  Suspension  or  Disallowance  on  these
transactions. Likewise, required documents are still yet to be submitted.

      As  for  the  alleged  unutilized  construction  materials  from   the
footbridge project, an inventory of the same is yet to  be  submitted  since
it was noted that these were included in the cost of  footbridge  recognized
as infrastructure asset in the books of the agency.

Lastly, we emphasized that Management should give priority to projects  that
truly address the need of  the  community  for  deeply-rooted  developments.
Further, implementation of these projects should be in accordance  with  the
approved AIP, and compliant with the  requirements  of  R.A.  9184.  It  was
reiterated that the negligent utilization of the 20% DF may  subject  erring
officials to penalites as provided in the above-mentioned JMC.

Recommendations:

We recommended to  direct  the  OIC-  Municipal  Accountant  to  maintain  a
special account in the General Fund for the 20%  Development  Fund,  prepare
the quarterly and annual Fund  Receipt  and  Utilization  Report  for  Other
Special Accounts, and submit the FRUR to the Office of  the  Auditor  on  or
before the 15th day of the month after each quarter.

Management’s Comments:

The Municipal Accountant assured us that a special account for 20%  DF  will
be created in the current year to  provide  sufficient  information  on  the
allocation and utilization of the fund.  Further,  the  office  was  already
aware of such requirement but failed to comply as the  audit  team  had  not
commented on it in prior years.

Recommendations:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require  the  Municipal  Development
Council (MDC) to specifically describe or  itemize  the  programs,  projects
and activities for inclusion  in  the  Annual  Investment  Plan.  Otherwise,
secure the approval or authorization of the Sangguniang  Bayan  (SB)  before
incurring expenditures and/or entering into contract in  the  implementation
of projects described in generic terms. Further, during  planning,  the  MDC
shall ensure that PPAs  for  inclusion  in  the  AIP  are  based  on  actual
proposals from different stakeholders with cost estimates that are based  on
approved program of works.

Management’s Comments:

Management acknowledged that there were indeed deficiencies during  planning
particularly in  identifying  the  particular  projects  to  be  implemented
during the year, and in disbursing  the  funds  without  prior  approval  or
authorization from the Sangguniang Bayan. Nonetheless,  the  OIC-  MBO  gave
her assurance that the audit recommendations will be complied  with  in  the
ensuing year.

Recommendations:

We recommended to the Management the following courses of action:

1. Refrain from using the 5% LDRRMF  for  expenditure  items  that  are  not
   directly related to disaster  prevention  and  mitigation,  preparedness,
   response, and rehabilitation and recovery;
2. Provide adequate funding  for  the  regular  operating  expenses  of  the
   MDRRMO particularly for the repair and maintenance of motor vehicles;


3. Submit a written explanation on the charges made  to  LDRRMF,  which  are
   not in line with PPAs mentioned in RA 10121 and NDRRMC-DBM-DILG  JMC  No.
   2013-1; and

4. Direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately  submit  the  lacking  DVs
   and supporting documents related to LDRRMF to the Office of the  Auditor,
   to preclude disallowances/suspensions in the audit.

Management’s Comments:

Management disbursed the LDRRM funds under the premise that  the  identified
PPAs are proper considering that the LDDMRF Plan  was  already  reviewed  by
the Office of the Civil Defence, Regional Office. In fact,  a  certification
was issued by the OCD that the PPAs are  aligned  wih  the  Provincial  DRRM
Plan. Further, the MDRRMO was tasked by the  Municipal  Mayor  to  spearhead
the operations of the Emergency Operation Center  and  to  provide  for  the
requirements of the operations to be charged under the LDRRMF.

As for the inclusion of the budget for the repair and maintenance of  rescue
vehicles  and  other  government  vehicles  used  to  address  the  disaster
response, under the LDRRMF,  the MDRRMO reasoned that  these  vehicles  were
in disaster response and other rescue  activities,  service  vehicle  during
vaccination  drives  to  various  barangays,  transport   of   patients   to
hospitals, fetching of OFWs and other requests.  In  addition,  although  the
office proposed a budget for this purpose under the MOOE allocation  of  the
office, this was not approved during the budget hearing.

For the payment of financial assistance to  fire  victims,  the  transaction
documents were prepared by the MSWDO and the beneficiaries were verified  to
be actual victims of the fire incident and required immediate assistance  to
support their shelter and basic needs.  Moreover,  the  MDRRMO  resorted  to
reimbursement as a mode of payment since a cash advance was not  immediately
granted in the 1st quarter of the  year  for  the  immediate  needs  of  the
agency as a result of the significant increase in COVID-19 cases during  the
period. The same instance occurred in the month  of  September  due  to  the
delay in the approval of the Supplemental Budget.


Auditor’s Rejoinder:

We reiterated that a  budget  for  the  repair  and  maintenance  of  rescue
vehicles should be provided under the MOOE allocation of the office  as  the
said vehicles assigned to the MDRRMO are still subject to regular upkeep  as
a result of wear and tear regardless of whether the same has been  used  for
disaster  response  or  other   administrative   activities.   Further,   we
reiterated that financial assistance for  fire  victims  should  be  charged
under the Assistance  for  Individuals  in  Crisis  (AICS)  Program  of  the
Municipality as the fire incident was not considered  a  disaster  affecting
the functioning of the community and did not  involve  widespread  human  or
economic loss.

Finally, we emphasized that the use of the LDRRMF for expenses that  may  be
charged to other appropriate sources of funds affects  the  availability  of
funds for the implementation of PPAs that would strengthen the capacity  for
disaster risk reduction and management of the Municipality.

Recommendations:

We recommended that the Municipal Disaster  Risk  Reduction  and  Management
Council (MDRRMC) follow the prescribed format of the  LDRMMFIP  as  provided
in the aforementioned COA Circular, and  ensure  that  identified  PAPs  and
their expected outputs are specific and measurable to aid in the  evaluation
of program implementation.

Management’s Comments:

The format being used by the MDRRMO in the preparation of the  LDRRMFIP  was
based on the prescribed format of the OCD.  Subsequent  preparation  of  the
LDRRMFIP shall henceforth be in accordance with  the  format  as  prescribed
under COA Circular 2012-002.

Recommendation:

We recommended the following to Management:

1. Direct the MDRRMO to prepare the LDRRMP to cover new  programs,  projects
   and expenditure items or changes to existing budgetary items;

2. Require the Municipal Development Council  to  prepare  the  supplemental
   investment program for covid-19 related PPAs that  are  not  among  those
   included in the approved AIP; and

3. Submit the LDRRMP and Supplemental Investment Program to the  Sangguniang
   Bayan for adoption or approval.

Management’s Comments:

The MDRRMO explained that due to immediate need for fund allocation for  the
operations of the Temporary Treatment and Monitoring Facility, as  a  result
of the increase in COVID-19 cases, the  MDRRMC  convened  to  reprogram  the
PPAs in the LDRRMFIP  particularly  the  PPAs  which  were  not  implemented
during the year due to quarantine restrictions. Concerning this, the  MDRRMO
submitted a copy of MDRRMC  Resolution  No.  03-A-2021  dated  September  9,
2021, Appropriation Ordinance  No.  3-  2021  authorizing  the  supplemental
budget no. 03-2021, and MDC Executive Committee Resolution No. 03-2021.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

Despite the  submission  of  the  MDRRMC  Resolution  and  other  supporting
documents, it was noted that the revised LDRRMFIP was not submitted and  the
Supplemental  Investment  Program,  although  approved  by   the   Municipal
Development  Council,  was  not  submitted  to  the  Sangguniang  Bayan  for
adoption or approval. While it is understood that the  funds  were  utilized
for DRRM purpose and given the urgency of the situation, we reiterated  that
the proper budgeting procedures must still be complied with as  a  necessary
control to prevent the unauthorized and improper use of said funds.

Recommendations:

We recommended to the BAC the following:

1. Prepare the bidding documents for every procurement  transaction  in  the
   standard form and  contents  as  prescribed  by  GPPB.  Submit  the  same
   together with advance copies of the PO and contracts in  compliance  with
   COA Circular 2009-001.

2. Conduct the pre-procurement for procurement transactions with ABC of  P2M
   and P5M for goods and infrastructure  projects,  respectively.  Likewise,
   conduct the pre-bid conference for contracts to be bid  with  an  ABC  of
   P1M. Comply with the prescribed timeline on the  conduct  of  procurement
   activities. Further, prepare the minutes of meeting for every procurement
   activity conducted  by  the  BAC  including  the  opening  of  bids,  bid
   evaluation and post-qualification. These shall be submitted together with
   the complete bidding documents.

3. In addition to posting the ITB  in  PhilGEPS,  the  same  shall  also  be
   posted in a conspicuous  place  in  the  Municipality  within  seven  (7)
   calendar days. The BAC Secretariat shall issue a certification  for  this
   purpose. Further, publish the NOAs and NTPs on the  PhilGEPS  website  in
   compliance with the transparency provision.

4. Require the posting of bid security before the signing  of  the  contract
   of agreement. In case of refusal  or  delay  of  the  winning  bidder  in
   posting the performance security, the BAC shall:


a. Forfeit the bid security and impose sanctions as provided in the IRR;

b. Disqualify the said bidder and initiate  the  post-qualification  process
   on the bidder with the second LCB.

5. Require the posting of performance security upon contract signing.

6.  Submit  the  following  documents  for  the  above-mentioned  goods  and
   infrastructure projects procured through public bidding:

a. Complete bidding documents

b. Eligibility documents and eligibility requirements

c. BAC Security (if any)
d. Minutes of meeting, BAC attendance sheet  and  invitation  to  three  (3)
   observers for every procurement activity

e. Certification from the BAC Secretariat on the posting of the ITB

a. BAC Resolution on the preliminary examination of bids
b. Minutes of BAC meeting on the detailed bid evaluation
           c. TWG Report on the Detailed Bid Evaluation  for  determination
              of the LCB, as reviewed by the BAC
d. Post-qualification Evaluation Report by the TWG
e. Notice of Post-qualification
f. Performance Security

   If the aforesaid reports and documents  were  not  prepared  or  are  not
   available, submit a written justification for us to evaluate  whether  to
   suspend or disallow the transaction.

7. For transactions using the alternative mode of procurement,  require  the
suppliers to submit complete documentary requirements  as  prescribed  in
Annex A of the IRR. With this, the agency shall  ensure  that  goods  are
procured from duly registered suppliers.

8. Section 11.2 of the IRR of RA  9184  requires  that  the  designated  BAC
members shall have unquestionable integrity and procurement  proficiency.
In this light, the BAC is enjoined to attend trainings and seminars on RA
9184 and be updated with the latest GPPB issuances.

Management’s Comments:

The Former BAC Chairman agreed that there were indeed lapses in the  conduct
of procurement for  projects  under  the  20%  development  fund  and  other
locally funded projects  and  programs.  Nonetheless,  these  projects  were
fully implemented.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

It was  emphasized  that  the  rules  and  procedures  in  the  conduct  of
procurement transactions must be strictly complied with to ensure that  the
government does not incur a loss in any transaction. Further,  Management’s
failure to submit the required justifications and documents relative to the
conduct of procurement activities warrants the  issuance  of  a  Notice  of
Suspension on these transactions.

Recommendations:

We recommended that management strictly complies with the provisions of  the
Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and COA  Circular  2009-001,  specifically  on  the
following:

1. Require all  the  end-users  to  submit  their  PPMP  for  the  year  and
   consolidate the same into an APP. Update  the  PPMP  and  APP  every  six
   months or as often as required by the HOPE. Ensure that all  procurements
   to be undertaken are in accordance with the approved APP.

2. Ensure that bid documents are prepared in accordance  with  the  standard
   forms and contents  prescribed  by  the  GPPB  and  submit  the  same  as
   prescribed under Section 17 of RA 9184;

3. Invite COA and two (2) observers in various  stages  of  the  procurement
   process, and submit the received copies of  invitation  to  observers  as
   part of the supporting documents to the approved contract agreement;

   documents to the interested bidders;

6. Submit a written  justification  for  the  failure  to  comply  with  the

Management’s Comments:

The Former BAC Chairman, in his reply to the AOM, admitted that  there  were
indeed lapses in the conduct of procurement for fuel, oil,  and  lubricants,
and other supplies and materials for the COVID-19 response  of  the  agency,
as the Management is deeply committed to address the COVID-19 pandemic.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

We reiterate that, notwithstanding the urgency of any given situation,  the
rules and procedures in the conduct of  procurement  transactions  must be
strictly complied with to ensure that the government does not incur a  loss
in any transaction. Further, Management’s failure to  submit  the  required
justifications  and  documents  relative  to  the  conduct  of  procurement
activities warrants the  issuance  of  a  Notice  of  Suspension  on  these
transactions.

Recommendations:

We recommended to Management the following:

     1. Require the sale of bidding documents to suppliers and  contractors
        to provide adequate funds for the honoraria of the BAC;


     2. Stop the practice of charging BAC honoraria to the approved  budget
        of the agency. The same shall  be  sourced  from  collections  from
        successfully completed procurement projects  or  savings  from  the
        local budget approved by the local sanggunian;

     3. Direct the Municipal Accountant to  review  the  propriety  of  the
        claims for BAC honoraria by requiring that the  same  be  supported
        with   copies   of   Purchase   Orders/Contracts   of   procurement
        transactions successfully completed during the period;


     4. Direct the BAC to comply with the rates prescribed  in  the  budget
        circular in the computation of its honoraria for the period; and

     5. Direct the Municipal Accountant to review the claims for  honoraria
        ensuring that these do not exceed 25% of the monthly basic salary.
Lastly, require the BAC to submit a justification  for  the  above-mentioned
deficiencies including copies of  POs/Contracts  of  successfully  completed
procurement  projects,  for  us  to  evaluate  whether   to   disallow   the
transactions.

Management’s Comments:

The OIC - Municipal Accountant averred that the payments  of  BAC  Honoraria
were not in accordance with the provisions of Budget  Circular  No.  2004-5a
and 2007-3.

Recommendations:

We recommended to Management the following:

1. Require the Municipal Accountant to recognize the expenses  on  the  year
   for which  these  were  incurred  following  the  matching  principle  of
   accounting. At the end of the year where payments are  yet  to  be  made,
   said obligations shall be recorded  as  expenses  and  the  corresponding
   Accounts Payable shall also be recognized. In this light,  management  is
   also reminded that the incurrence of expenses shall be within  the  limit
   of the current appropriations and to refrain from utilizing  the  current
   year’s  appropriations  for  the  payment  of  prior  year’s   unrecorded
   obligations so as not  to  deplete  the  funds  for  the  current  year’s
   expenses.

2. Direct the BAC and all end-users to ensure that the rentals of any  motor
   vehicles are in accordance with the requirements of R.A. 9184.

3. Consider the allocation of funds for  the  provision  of  motor  vehicles
   that may be utilized for the regular activities and operations of various
   offices of the Municipality as this would be more economical in the  long
   run as compared to the periodic rental of transport vehicles.

4. Provide a justification/explanation for the above-mentioned  deficiencies
   for us to evaluate whether to suspend or disallow the transactions.

Management’s Comments:

As explained by the Former OIC Accountant, hiring of vehicles has long  been
practiced  by  the  agency  particularly   during   emergency   and   urgent
situations.  Breanna  Lynda  and  Camille  Transport  Service  is  the  only
registered van owner in the Municipality,  thus,  on  occasions  when  their
vans are not available, Management hired non-registered  vans.  As  for  the
varying cost of hiring van, this depends on  the  number  of  days  the  van
stayed in the point  of  destination.  For  the  hiring  of  truck  for  the
delivery of relief goods, the terms of delivery were not  indicated  in  the
contract, thus, the Accounting Office assumed that the  transaction  was  in
order.

Further,  the  prior  year’s  expenses   were   charged   to   the   current
appropriations as there were  no  funds  in  prior  years  for  the  payment
thereof. Nonetheless, Management assured  us  that  subsequent  transactions
will be in accordance with R.A. 9184.

Auditor’s Rejoinder:

Regardless of the mode of procurement resorted to by  Management,  the  most
advantageous  price  for  the  government  shall  be   obtained   in   every

procurement activities,  including  those  using  the  alternative  mode  of
procurement,  shall  be  strictly  complied  with.  Failure  to  submit  the
complete documentary requirements relative to  the  conduct  of  procurement
shall warrant the issuance of a Notice of Suspension on the transactions.

Recommendation:


      We recommended that management enforce effective and efficient control
   measures to ensure the timely turnover of collections  from  collectors  and
   tellers to the Municipal Treasurer. Further, direct the Municipal  Treasurer
   to deposit all collections intact and regularly to the  AGDB  to  avoid  the
   accumulation of undeposited collections at the month-end.

Recommendation:


      We recommended that management enforce the  immediate  liquidation  of
   the outstanding cash advances through the issuance of letters of  demand  to
   the  officials  and  employees  concerned,  and  strictly  comply  with  the
   provisions  of  COA  Circular  97-002  in  the  granting,  utilization   and
   liquidation of cash advances.

Recommendation:


      We recommended that  management  issue  immediately  an  office  order
   designating the aforesaid three (3) AOs as special disbursing  officers  and
   ensure compliance with the bonding requirements.

Recommendation:

       We recommended  that  OIC-Municipal  Accountant  make  the  necessary
   adjusting entries to reflect the correct  balance  of  the  Loans  Payable-
   Domestic account. Further, install controls that  ensure  the  accuracy  of
   journal entries prepared by the Accounting staff.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that management enforce the  immediate  liquidation  of
   these fund transfers or resort to legal remedies if warranted,  pursuant  to
   Section 9 of COA Circular 2007-001.

Recommendations:


      As a reiteration of prior year’s audit recommendations, we recommended
   to the Management the following courses of action:


     1. Direct the Inventory/Property Committee to conduct an effective and
        complete physical inventory of all PPE items  of  the  Municipality
        and prepare the corresponding RPCPPE with remarks on the  condition
        of the listed equipment.


     2. Reconcile the results thereof with the available accounting records
        maintained  by  the  Municipal  Accounting  Office   to   determine
        unrecorded properties and other differences for further validation.
        Thereafter, submit a report on the results of  reconciliation  made
        for review and verification.


     3.  Maintain  PPE  ledger  cards  and  property  cards  to  facilitate
        efficient reconciliation of physical count  versus  accounting  and
        property records.


     4. Provide necessary adjusting entries in the  books  of  accounts  if
        deemed necessary.

Recommendation:

      We recommended  that  adjusting  entries  be  made  by  the  Municipal
   Accountant in the General Fund books to  reflect  the  correct  PPE  account
   balance as at year-end.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that MACCO review the  details  of  the  aforementioned
   deficiencies in coordination with the project  implementers  (MEO)  for  the
   status of various PPAs and provide adjustments  in  the  books  to  properly
   reclassify the accounts  and  balances.  Further,  compute  and  record  the
   related depreciation expenses to reflect the correct  PPE  carrying  amounts
   at year-end.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that management itemize the  specific  projects  to  be
   funded during the year. Otherwise, secure authorization  of  the  SB  before
   the procurement of goods and services  for  projects  not  specified  in  the
   appropriation ordinance.

Recommendations:

      We recommended to the management the following courses of action:


     a. The Local Development Council (LDC) shall ensure that only priority
        development projects that are in accordance with the guidelines  on
        the use of the fund will be included in the plan.  For  PPAs  which
        are inconsistent with the guidelines, initiate amendments such that
        the actual development needs of the constituents are addressed; and


     b. Design and established control procedures to  ensure  that  funds  are
        used for its intended purpose.

a)  Utilize  the  available  balances  of   LDRRMF,   including   unexpended

   DVs and supporting documents related to  LDRRMF  to  the  Office  of  the
   Auditor, to preclude disallowances/suspensions in the audit.

   guidelines, and to prioritize the welfare of its constituents  by  providing

   the funds received  from  other  agencies  for  its  intended  purpose.  The
   Management is also enjoined to submit a  written  explanation  on  the  said
   fund misuse and plan of action to rectify the said audit observation.

   Circular No. 2012-002. Further, SLs shall be maintained for transfers  of

   Sources and Utilization of DRRMF following  the prescribed  format  by  COA
   Circular No. 2012-002, to be certified  by  the  Municipal  Accountant,  and
   timely submit the same to the Office of the Auditor on or  before  the  15th
   day after the end of each month through the LDRRMC and LDC.

        assignments and to ensure that actual accomplishments are  relevant

        that are specific in terms of actual  duties  performed  such  that
        proper evaluation may be made on the necessity of hiring/re-hiring.

Recommendations:

1. Impose control measures to enforce compliance with  tax  regulations  and
   ensure proper declaration and payment of correct  taxes  due  to  BIR  to
   avoid deficiency tax assessments.

2. Submit a written justification on the deficiency and action plan  on  how
   to recover the said government losses.

Recommendations:

a. Create or strengthen the LGU GFPS in accordance with MCW-IRR;
b. Establish and maintain a functional  GAD  Database  which  shall  aid  in

c. Ensure that identified PPAs address emerging  gender-related  issues  and
   concerns.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that management submit a written justification for  the
   aforesaid deficiencies. Further, ensure that a  specific  appropriation  for
   POP is included in the Annual Budget.

Recommendations:

      We recommended for management to properly  plan  and  monitor  project
   timelines to facilitate the conduct of procurement activities.


      Further, we recommended that  management  discontinue  the  use  of  a
   “pakyaw contract” in the implementation of various  projects  as  this  does
   not  meet  the  requirements  of  R.A.  9184  and  to   submit   a   written
   justification for the aforesaid deficiencies to enable us to decide  on  the
   audit whether to suspend/disallow the transactions.

Recommendations:

      We recommend that management strictly complies with the provisions  of
   the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184 and COA Circular 2009-001, specifically on  the
   following:


a.  The  BAC  is  enjoined  to  attend  various  training  and  seminars  on
   procurement.
b. BAC should prepare and submit APP to DBM and COA in accordance  with  the
   required timeline;
c. Ensure that bid documents are prepared in accordance  with  the  standard
   forms and contents prescribed by the GPPB;
d. Invite COA and two (2) observers in various  stages  of  the  procurement
   process, and submit the received copies of  invitation  to  observers  as
   part of the supporting documents to the approved contract agreement;
e. Ensure that the  prospective  bidders  submit  the  complete  eligibility
   requirements and post the necessary bid security;
f. Require the posting of performance security upon contract signing;
g. Publish the NOAs and NTPs in the PhilGEPS website in compliance with  the
   transparency provision;
h. Submit the contracts/POs and its supporting  documents  within  five  (5)
   working days from the execution thereof;
i. Ensure the timely preparation and submission of the PMR to  GPPB  and  to
   copy furnish the COA; and
j. Submit a written justification for the aforesaid deficiencies  to  enable
   us to make a decision in the audit on  whether  to  suspend/disallow  the
   transactions.

Recommendations:

      We recommended that management discontinue the payment of honoraria on
   the basis of meetings conducted and ensure that the  sources  of  funds  for
   these claims are those specified in BC No. 2007-03.


      Further, capacitate the BAC by requiring  attendance  to  seminars  on
   procurement and require prospective bidders to pay a fee  for  the  cost  of
   bidding documents such that the proceeds may be used to pay  for  the  BAC’s
   honoraria.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that management discontinue the use of public funds for
   private and religious purposes.  Further,  submit  a  written  justification
   for the aforesaid deficiencies to enable  us  to  decide  in  the  audit  on
   whether to suspend/disallow the transactions.

        Management is  reminded  to  prioritize  the  construction  of  the
        sanitary landfill as a final disposal site;


     3. Reconstitute the MSWMB and ensure that the responsibilities of  the
        members are properly carried out;


     4. Revisit the provisions of the Municipal Ordinance, particularly  on
        the collection of garbage  fees,  propose  necessary  revisions  if
        warranted, and ensure proper dissemination;


     5. Give emphasis during IEC on the critical role of  stakeholders  and
        barangays in the full implementation of the ESWM Plan  by  offering
        incentives or imposing sanctions pursuant to Section 45 and  50  of
        RA 9003.
